programs.name: Medical Assistance/Medicaid
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Illinois Medicaid can cover nursing home care or in-home services.

localEntities.name: Illinois Department of Human Services Family Community Resource Centers (FCRCs)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and struggling to afford long-term care, Illinois Medicaid covers nursing home care, in-home personal care, adult day care, meal delivery, and home modifications. Because Medicaid is designed for people with limited income and assets, your parent must meet strict financial limits and demonstrate they need help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or managing medications.

The program has different tracks: nursing home Medicaid provides immediate full coverage if your parent qualifies, while home-based services through AABD Medicaid are provided one at a time based on clinical assessment. Income limits range from $1,304-$1,330/month for seniors, and asset limits vary from $2,000-$17,500 depending on which program track they qualify for.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if spouse lives there or equity ≤$752,000)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, or medication management)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Illinois Web Benefits or call your local Family Community Resource Center.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect Social Security statements, pension records, bank statements for the past 3 months, and property deeds.

steps.title: Complete medical assessment
steps.description: Your parent will need a functional assessment to determine what level of care they need for daily activities.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online through Illinois Web Benefits (ABE) at hfs.illinois.gov or call your local FCRC office.

steps.title: Attend interview
steps.description: Complete required interview with FCRC caseworker to review eligibility and care needs.

applicationGuide.waitlist: AABD benefits are assessed one at a time (not immediate like nursing home coverage)
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's assets are slightly over the limit, speak with an elder law attorney about spend-down strategies before applying.

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.description: Full nursing home coverage with immediate benefits
tiers.coverage: Complete nursing home care, medical services, prescriptions

tiers.name: AABD Medicaid
tiers.description: Home and community-based services provided one at a time
tiers.coverage: In-home personal care, adult day care, meal delivery, home modifications, emergency response systems

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if spouse or disabled child lives there, or equity ≤$752,000)

contentSections.text: AABD benefits are provided one at a time based on assessment, not immediately like nursing home coverage. Your parent may qualify for some services but not others.
Asset limits vary dramatically by program type: $2,000 for AABD vs. $17,500 for nursing home Medicaid. Apply for the right track based on your parent's care needs and financial situation.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your mom or a spouse lives in the home, or if the home's equity is $752,000 or less in 2026. The home is exempt from asset limits in these cases.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: If your dad's income exceeds the limit, he may still qualify by spending down on medical expenses or through special income trusts. Consult an elder law attorney about options.

faqs.question: Does Mom's savings account count toward the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, bank accounts and savings are counted toward the asset limit. She'll need to be under $2,000-$17,500 depending on which Medicaid program she applies for.

faqs.question: How quickly can Dad get home care services through Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Nursing home coverage starts immediately if approved, but AABD home services are assessed and provided one at a time. The timeline isn't specified but expect it to take longer than nursing home placement.

faqs.question: Will Medicaid cover all of Mom's medical expenses?
faqs.answer: Medicaid covers physician visits, prescriptions, emergency room visits, and hospital care, plus long-term care services like personal care and meal delivery based on her assessed needs.

faqs.question: What happens to Dad's Social Security if he gets Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Your dad keeps his Social Security, but most of it may go toward his cost of care if he's in a nursing home. He'll keep a small personal needs allowance (typically around $30-60/month).

programs.name: Home Services Program (HSP) Waiver
programs.tagline: Get paid caregivers and adult day care if your disabled adult child (under 60) needs daily help at home instead of a nursing facility.
programs.intro:
If your adult child has severe disabilities and is under 60, Illinois HSP Waiver can provide paid personal assistants, adult day care, and daily living support at home. Because HSP covers the cost of home-based care that would otherwise require nursing home placement, your family member can stay home while getting professional help with bathing, dressing, medication management, and household tasks.

Your adult child must have assets below $17,500 and score at least 29 on Illinois' clinical assessment (Determination of Need). Because HSP has limited enrollment slots, meeting eligibility requirements doesn't guarantee immediate services — waitlists exist when the program is full.

summary: At risk of nursing home placement
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must score at least 29 on Determination of Need assessment (measures daily living support needs)
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident
Applied for or eligible for Medicaid
Physician certifies home care is safe
Care costs same or less than nursing home

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Illinois Department of Rehabilitation Services directly — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: You must apply for Medicaid or prove eligibility for Medicaid spend-down before HSP approval (interim services available during processing).

steps.title: Contact DRS
steps.description: Call Illinois Department of Rehabilitation Services to request HSP Waiver application — they will schedule your Determination of Need assessment.

steps.title: Get physician certification
steps.description: Your doctor must certify that your family member needs long-term care and that it can be safely provided at home.

steps.title: Complete DON assessment
steps.description: DRS will conduct clinical assessment to determine if your family member scores 29+ on functional needs evaluation.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Limited enrollment slots — eligibility doesn't guarantee immediate services
applicationGuide.tip: Gather asset documentation early — the $17,500 limit is strictly enforced

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of U.S. citizenship or lawful permanent resident status

categories.name: Financial
items: Medicaid application or proof of eligibility

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing severe disability lasting 12+ months
Physician certification letter for home-based long-term care

contentSections.text: HSP is NOT for elderly parents. This program serves people under 60 with disabilities. If your parent is 65+, apply for the 'Persons who are Elderly Waiver' instead.
Spouses and parents of minor children cannot be paid as HSP caregivers, but other family members can receive payment for providing care services.

faqs.question: Can my 25-year-old son keep his apartment if he qualifies for HSP?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your son's primary residence doesn't count toward the $17,500 asset limit. He can keep his home and still qualify for HSP services.

faqs.question: What if my daughter has $20,000 in savings — is she automatically disqualified?
faqs.answer: Yes. The $17,500 asset limit for adults 18+ is a hard cap. She would need to spend down or transfer assets to qualify, but consult with DRS about proper procedures.

faqs.question: Can I be paid to care for my adult disabled son through HSP?
faqs.answer: Only if you're not his spouse and he's not a minor child. Other family members like siblings, adult children, or extended family can be paid HSP caregivers.

faqs.question: What happens if my son scores 28 on the assessment instead of 29?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify for HSP. The Determination of Need score of at least 29 is a strict requirement — there's no appeal process for scores just below the threshold.

faqs.question: How long will we wait for services after approval?
faqs.answer: Unknown. HSP has limited enrollment slots, so approval doesn't guarantee immediate services. Illinois doesn't publish current waitlist times.

faqs.question: Does my daughter need to be on Medicaid before applying for HSP?
faqs.answer: She needs to apply for Medicaid or be eligible for Medicaid spend-down. Interim HSP services can start during Medicaid processing, but ongoing services require Medicaid connection.

programs.name: Illinois PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, meals, transportation, and support under one team.

localEntities.name: West Chicago, South Chicago, Southern Cook County, Peoria, East St. Louis

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs extensive daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, Illinois PACE might be the answer. PACE provides everything — primary care, specialists, medications, meals, transportation, home care, even adult day programs — all coordinated by one team at no cost if your parent qualifies for Medicaid.

Because PACE replaces all other Medicare and Medicaid coverage, your parent gets seamless care without juggling multiple providers, insurance approvals, or coverage gaps. The catch: PACE is only available in specific zip codes in 5 Illinois regions, and your parent must qualify for nursing home-level care while being safe to live in the community with support.

For dual-eligible participants (Medicare + Medicaid), PACE is completely free. Private pay costs $7,000+ per month, making Medicaid qualification essential for most families.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing home level of care (requiring extensive daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management) but able to live safely in the community with PACE support
otherRequirements: Live in specific PACE service area zip codes
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Search by zip code first to confirm eligibility, then contact your local PACE provider for assessment — processing time varies by region.

steps.title: Check if you're in a PACE service area
steps.description: Search by zip code at hfs.illinois.gov/medicalproviders/pace.html — PACE is only available in West Chicago, South Chicago, Southern Cook County, Peoria, and East St. Louis regions

steps.title: Contact your local PACE organization
steps.description: Call the PACE provider in your area for initial screening and to schedule a comprehensive assessment

steps.title: Complete nursing home level of care certification
steps.description: Illinois will assess whether your parent meets the clinical criteria for nursing home care while being safe to live in the community

steps.title: Financial eligibility review
steps.description: If seeking free coverage, complete Medicaid application through Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible waitlist varies by region and provider
applicationGuide.tip: Start with the zip code search — if your parent doesn't live in a PACE service area, they cannot enroll regardless of other qualifications.

urls.label: Find PACE providers by zip code

contentSections.heading: Medicaid Income Limits for Free PACE Coverage
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits. PACE enrollment itself has no income limits, but Medicaid qualification needed for free coverage.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits for Medicaid: What Counts
excluded: Burial plots and prepaid funeral plans

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of address in PACE service area

categories.name: Medical Records
items: Recent hospital or nursing home records

contentSections.text: PACE completely replaces your parent's existing Medicare and Medicaid coverage. They cannot keep their current doctors or Medicare Advantage plan while enrolled in PACE.
Private pay PACE costs $7,000+ per month. If your parent's income is over Medicaid limits, consider Medicaid planning with an elder law attorney before applying.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her current doctors if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE replaces all Medicare and Medicaid coverage, so your mom will receive all care through the PACE team. She cannot keep outside doctors or specialists while enrolled in PACE.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: He can still enroll in PACE but will pay a share of cost ($200-$900/month) or full private pay ($7,000+/month). Consider consulting an elder law attorney about Medicaid planning to qualify for free coverage.

faqs.question: Can Mom disenroll from PACE if she doesn't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE enrollment is voluntary and your mom can disenroll at any time. She would then return to regular Medicare coverage and need to find new providers.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover nursing home care if Dad needs it temporarily?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE covers all Medicare and Medicaid services, including temporary nursing home stays, hospital care, and rehabilitation. The PACE team coordinates all care levels.

faqs.question: What if we move to a different county in Illinois?
faqs.answer: Your parent can only stay in PACE if moving within the same PACE service area. Moving to a county without PACE means they must disenroll and find new coverage.

faqs.question: How do we know if Mom qualifies for nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: Illinois conducts a clinical assessment to determine if your mom needs extensive daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management — the same level of care provided in nursing homes.
